structure.title: Fire in the Wildland-Urban Interface: Selecting Firewise Shrubs to Reduce Wildfire Risk
structure.summary:
This partial document, FOR272, focuses on reducing wildfire risk in the Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI) by guiding homeowners, developers, and individuals on selecting and maintaining "firewise" shrubs for landscaping. It explains that being firewise involves using low- to moderate-flammability shrubs and outlines characteristics that contribute to a shrub's flammability (e.g., leaf shape, water content, chemical traits).

The document highlights research that categorized 34 common landscaping shrubs into high, moderate, and low flammability, recommending low-flammability species for planting near homes and advising caution or avoidance for others. It also stresses that even firewise plants can become flammable during droughts and should never be planted directly against structures. Crucially, it emphasizes the importance of routine maintenance within the defensible space (at least 100 feet from structures), including removing dead material and pruning to prevent shrubs from acting as "ladder fuels" that can spread fire to treetops. The text also provides links to more detailed guides and related fact sheets in the "Fire in the Wildland-Urban Interface" series.

structure.title: Other Fact Sheets in the “Fire in the Wildland-Urban Interface” Series
structure.summary: This partial document lists various fact sheets within the "Fire in the Wildland-Urban Interface" series. The main points covered by these fact sheets include selecting Firewise shrubs and plants to reduce wildfire risk, considering fire in Florida's ecosystems, preparing Firewise plant lists for residents, reducing wildfire risk while achieving other landscaping goals, selecting and maintaining Firewise plants, and understanding fire behavior. It also provides links to where these fact sheets can be accessed.
